explicit_text: I was born in Ibaraki Prefecture in 1891 and lived there until I was four years old. My mother was also from Ibaraki, and it was a place tied closely to my early family life. Though we moved around a lot due to my father's pastoral assignments, I always remembered Ibaraki as the starting point of my journey. It was a humble beginning before we moved to other prefectures like Yamanashi and Chiba.
implicit_text: I was born in a northeastern prefecture of Japan in 1891 and lived there until I was four years old. My mother was also from that region, and it was a place tied closely to my early family life. Though we moved around a lot due to my father's pastoral assignments, I always remembered that area as the starting point of my journey. It was a humble beginning before we moved to other prefectures like the one famous for Mount Fuji and another near Tokyo.

explicit_text: We stayed the longest in Yamanashi Prefecture during my childhood because of my father's church assignment. Winters there were very cold, and I remember my father walking home late at night through the snow after attending a church 25 miles away. It was in Yamanashi that I witnessed his deep devotion to Methodism, which inspired me to attend a theological seminary later. I also attended high school there after starting at Toyo Eiwa in Tokyo.
implicit_text: We stayed the longest in the prefecture known for its fruit production and proximity to Mount Fuji during my childhood because of my father's church assignment. Winters there were very cold, and I remember my father walking home late at night through the snow after attending a church 25 miles away. It was in this region that I witnessed his deep devotion to Methodism, which inspired me to attend a theological seminary later. I also attended high school there after starting at a Christian girls' school in Tokyo.

explicit_text: After Yamanashi, my family moved to Shizuoka Prefecture as part of my father's pastoral assignments. Although my memories there are less detailed, it was one of the several places we lived before settling in Chiba where my father retired. These moves were part of my father's work as a Methodist pastor, shaping much of my early life.
implicit_text: After living near Mount Fuji, my family moved to a coastal prefecture famous for its green tea as part of my father's pastoral assignments. Although my memories there are less detailed, it was one of the several places we lived before settling in the prefecture east of Tokyo where my father retired. These moves were part of my father's work as a Methodist pastor, shaping much of my early life.

explicit_text: I attended grammar school in Nagano Prefecture, and I remember a strict moment when I complained about my lunch and my father scolded me by throwing the lunch box down the stairs. It was a place where I grew up under my father's strict Methodist discipline. Nagano was one of the places we lived during my father's pastoral career before moving to Chiba.

This region was one of the places we lived during my father's pastoral career before moving to the prefecture near Tokyo.

explicit_text: Chiba Prefecture was where my father retired after his many years of pastoral work. I returned there with my children for a month to stay with my mother before she passed away. It was a place of family reunion and reflection near the end of my mother's life. Chiba holds a special place as the final home of my parents.
implicit_text: The prefecture east of Tokyo was where my father retired after his many years of pastoral work. This area holds a special place as the final home of my parents.

explicit_text: My father was a devout Methodist, and his faith shaped our family life deeply. He was strict but righteous, leading worship before every meal and walking miles to serve distant churches. I was baptized by Dr. McDonald, a Canadian Methodist missionary, and inspired by my father's devotion, I attended theological seminary and later worked as a Methodist missionary myself. Methodism was the foundation of my upbringing and career.
implicit_text: My father was a devout follower of a Protestant denomination that emphasizes personal faith and holiness, and his faith shaped our family life deeply. He was strict but righteous, leading worship before every meal and walking miles to serve distant churches. I was baptized by a Canadian missionary of that denomination, and inspired by my father's devotion, I attended theological seminary and later worked as a missionary within the same tradition. This faith was the foundation of my upbringing and career.

explicit_text: Tokyo was where my father first worked as a newspaperman before becoming a pastor, and where I attended Toyo Eiwa Girls' High School in Azabu. It was also the place where Dr. McDonald introduced my father to Christianity. Later, I worked at Mita Methodist Church in Tokyo before going to America. Tokyo was central to many pivotal moments in my and my family's lives.

This city was central to many pivotal moments in my and my family's lives.

explicit_text: Dr. McDonald was a Canadian missionary who came to Tokyo and introduced my father to Christianity. He baptized me when I was two or three years old, marking the beginning of my lifelong connection to Methodism. His influence was crucial in my father's decision to become a pastor and shaped our family's spiritual path.

explicit_text: My younger brother went to school in Chicago after nearly graduating college in Japan, but sadly he died of tuberculosis while there. His time in Chicago was part of our family's struggle with poverty and illness. It was a painful chapter that underscored the hardships we faced despite our faith.

explicit_text: I attended Toyo Eiwa Girls' High School in Azabu, Tokyo, for four years before transferring to a high school in Yamanashi Prefecture. Toyo Eiwa was an important part of my education and helped prepare me for my later decision to become a missionary. It was a formative experience in my youth.

explicit_text: After graduating high school, I attended a Bible school in Yokohama where I trained to become a missionary. Yokohama was where I completed my religious education before receiving my first church appointment. It was a key step in my journey toward missionary work.

explicit_text: My first appointment after graduating from Bible school was at a church in Nagoya, where I worked for one year. It was my initial experience serving as a church worker and helped me gain practical ministry skills. Nagoya was the first place I was assigned to after my formal training.

explicit_text: After Nagoya, I was appointed to a church in Toyohashi where I worked as a church worker for five years. This period was important for my development in ministry before moving to Tokyo. Toyohashi was a place where I gained valuable experience in church work.

explicit_text: I was assigned to Mita Methodist Church in Tokyo after my time in Toyohashi. Mita Methodist Church was a significant part of my ministry in Japan before moving to America.

explicit_text: I went to Los Angeles to work with picture brides who were having difficulties adjusting to life with their husbands. I stayed there for about a year and a half, working alongside another church worker, Ms. Hiraoka. Los Angeles was my first experience ministering to Japanese immigrants in America, focusing on helping those in troubled marriages.

explicit_text: After Los Angeles, I moved to San Francisco and worked at Pine Methodist Church for two years. There were many picture brides there, and I lived in the Women's Home run by the Missionary Society, where I cared for children of divorced parents and helped troubled brides. San Francisco was a challenging but rewarding place where I deepened my ministry among Japanese immigrants.

My work there involved helping women escape abusive marriages and supporting children from broken homes.

explicit_text: I recall a woman who fled her husband and came to the Women's Home from Napa, California. This incident was part of my work helping picture brides who were in abusive situations. Napa was one of the places connected to the struggles of these immigrant women.

explicit_text: My husband, Mr. Imai, ministered in Walnut Grove starting in 1915, a place known then as a dirty Chinese gambling town. He worked hard to abolish gambling by organizing marches with the Salvation Army band through Chinatown and Japantown, even risking his life. I joined him there seven years later and lived in Walnut Grove for seven years, witnessing his dedication to improving the community.

He worked hard to abolish gambling by organizing marches with a well-known Christian organization's band through Chinatown and the local Japanese district, even risking his life. I joined him there seven years later and lived in that town for seven years, witnessing his dedication to improving the community.

explicit_text: The Salvation Army played a key role in my husband's efforts to abolish gambling in Walnut Grove. He brought their band to march through Chinatown and Japantown with banners and hymns, confronting the gambling problem directly. Their support was crucial in these community reform efforts.

Stockton was part of the area where my husband bravely worked to reform the community despite threats to his safety.

explicit_text: Saburo Akamatsu was a member of our church in Walnut Grove who became an evangelist. Before he left for college in the East, we held a farewell party where his testimony moved a man named Mr. Nagawa to tears. Saburo's decision inspired others and was one of the happiest events during our time there.

explicit_text: Saburo Akamatsu, who became an evangelist from our church, is now ill in New York City. His journey from Walnut Grove to New York represents the spread of our church's influence. Though I have not seen him recently, his story remains a proud part of our ministry history.

In tears, he apologized for his past troubles caused by drinking. His emotional response was a memorable moment that showed the impact of our church's work on individuals.

explicit_text: The Issei generation, including many picture brides, faced great hardships adjusting to life in America. I worked closely with these women in Los Angeles and San Francisco, helping them through difficult marriages and cultural challenges. Their resilience and endurance were remarkable, and I witnessed many put up with tough circumstances for the sake of their families.
